title: Anger at the Erasure of Indigenous Land
dek: The erasure of Indigenous land is not just a political issue; it is a biological reality felt in the psoas and the diaphragm. When the earth is paved over, the body holds the grief and rage of that loss, demanding to be heard.
bodyMarkdown:
You stand barefoot on the cracked earth, the late afternoon sun lowering behind a thin veil of smoke and heat haze, the sky a bruised lavender that smells faintly of cedar and ash. The ground beneath your feet is dry, the soil hard and unyielding, pressing into the pads of your toes like a silent accusation. You feel the weight of the land’s history press into your psoas, that deep core muscle that anchors you to the earth and carries the memory of every step your ancestors never took here. The air is thick with the scent of sage and dust, the kind that settles deep into your lungs, making the diaphragm clench involuntarily, as if it remembers a scream half-formed, swallowed by the centuries.

This is stolen ground. The grass that once waved freely under open skies is now a patchwork of fences and forgotten treaties, a landscape erased and rewritten by hands that never asked permission. Your body knows this as surely as your mind does. The anger that rises here is not some distant, intellectual outrage. It is a visceral tightening in the belly, a raw edge in the levator scapulae where your neck meets your shoulders, that sharp reminder that your presence here is an intrusion—because the land remembers, and so does your body.

You can feel it in the slow, stubborn knot in the trapezius, that muscle that carries the weight of unspoken histories, and in the subtle twitch of the masseter as your jaw clenches against the injustice. This is not a political abstraction discussed in quiet rooms or printed in sanitized reports. This is biology. This is the body remembering what the mind might try to forget: that anger is an embodied truth, a pulse beneath the skin that refuses to be ignored.

When the wind shifts, it carries whispers of old songs and mourning prayers, the scent of wild herbs burned in vigil, and the unmistakable bitterness of loss. Your breath catches, the diaphragm tightening like a drumskin pulled taut, announcing presence and resistance. This is the anger of survival, of lineage, of a body that cannot separate itself from the land it inhabits. You do not carry this anger lightly. It is a living thing, a fierce pulse in your cells, a quiet roar in the sinews, demanding to be recognized, to be honored.

Here, on this stolen earth, your fury is not ugly. It is necessary. It is the body insisting on truth.

## The Fascia Remembers What the Maps Forgot

You step onto the cracked pavement, the air dry and brittle just after dawn, the concrete gleaming under the sharp morning sun. Around you, the space is scrubbed clean, manicured lawns where wildflowers once dared to bloom, their colors traded for uniform green and sterile paths. The smell of crushed earth is gone, replaced by the faint chemical tang of pavement and weed killer. This isn’t just a stroll through a park; it’s a passage through erasure, a walking through the ghost of what was.

In your jaw, the masseter tightens without permission. It clamps down, a silent clench that speaks of long-buried rage and grief. The fascia there—dense, fibrous, unyielding—holds onto the memory of those wild places, the roots and the untamed undergrowth, the water that once danced freely beneath the canopy. The masseter is not just a muscle for chewing; it is a messenger of tension, and right now, it braces against the violence of loss. The body remembers what the maps have deliberately forgotten, the stories of land stolen, flattened, sanitized.

Your trapezius muscles flare next, the broad wings from neck to shoulders tightening, pulling the weight of history into your upper back. You can almost feel the levator scapulae whispering its own sadness, tugging at the base of your skull where the nerves converge and flare. This is not the mere physical fatigue of walking; it is the body’s way of saying no, a biological insistence that this erasure wounds deeply. The fascia here thickens, a web of connective tissue stiffening in mourning and resistance. It’s as if your body is bracing against the concrete itself, holding a lineage of fury and grief not just inside your mind but in every fiber of your being.

Anger is not a failure of the spirit or a moral lapse; it is a biological necessity. When the psoas contracts in fight or flight, when the diaphragm catches in a sharp intake of breath, when the masseter clenches and the trapezius tightens, your body is speaking a language older than words. This anger is a signal, a way to honor the land’s memory and your own. It is the body’s way of saying, “This should not be.” You might feel it as a heat radiating from the back of your neck, a dull ache beneath your jaw, a tightness that refuses to relent no matter how many times you tell yourself to relax. This is not pathology, this is biology.

In these moments, listen to the subtle voices in your fascia, the tight bands and knots that do not simply respond to your will but remember history. The body is a living archive, and your anger—held in muscle, tendon, and connective tissue—is a form of knowledge, a fierce remembrance of what was lost and a refusal to let that loss be erased again.

## Resmaa Menakem and the Settled Body

When Resmaa Menakem talks about trauma, he doesn’t just talk about stories or memories—he talks about the body, the raw, trembling, flesh-and-fascia vessel you inhabit every second. The body knows what the mind can’t always hold. Menakem’s work on somatic abolitionism is a fierce invitation to listen to your own muscle, your own nervous system, to understand that trauma isn’t lodged solely in history books or news reports but in the very tissues of your being. Trauma lives in your trapezius that tightens the moment you sense threat, in your diaphragm that clamps shut when fear grips you, in the masseter that clenches when words won’t come out right.

He unpacks how the erasure of Indigenous land is more than a physical loss—it's a tearing apart of the body’s roots, a severing of the deep tissue connections that tether you to place and lineage. There is a disembodiment that comes from stolen land, a disorientation in the body’s innate map. The body remembers the earth the way the jaw remembers clenched rage. When the land is taken, the body loses its ground, the fascia around your psoas feels loose and unstable, and the nervous system gets caught in a liminal space, neither safe nor free. This isn’t metaphor; it’s biology. The body does not forget the violence done to its geography.

Anger, then, becomes the body’s attempt to re-anchor itself. It is the raw energy coursing through the levator scapulae, the sharp flare in the occipital ridge, the heat rising in the skin at 3 a.m. on a humid summer night when the air is thick and your nerves are brittle. Anger is the body trying to say, “I am here. I am real. I am not erasing myself.” It’s the muscle’s way of demanding attention, of reclaiming space that’s been denied. This isn’t pathology or a moral failing; this is biology speaking in its native tongue. The body remembers what the mind sometimes refuses to see, and anger is its loud, unignorable dialect.

Menakem’s somatic abolitionism insists that healing from racialized trauma requires more than conversation or policy change; it requires the body to settle. To settle is to feel the ground beneath your feet, to soften the tension in the masseter and trapezius that have held the weight of centuries. It’s to breathe into the diaphragm with the slow, deliberate rhythm of a dusk breeze, to invite the nervous system out of its fight-or-flight stasis and into a place of recalibration. This is not about suppressing anger or softening pain until it’s pretty—it’s about meeting the body where it is, with unflinching respect and care, so that trauma’s grip can loosen, and the body can begin to remember how to be whole, how to be settled, how to be free.

## Gabor Maté and the Myth of Normal

You live in a world that insists on a version of “normal” so narrow it strangles the body before you even recognize the shape of your own breath. Gabor Maté, with his unflinching gaze on trauma, reminds us that what society calls normal is often a slow, collective wound. Trauma, in his view, is not just the dramatic episodes you might recall at midnight or in therapy sessions; it’s the quiet, relentless disconnection from your own nature, from the land beneath your feet, from the rhythm of your body’s breath and the pulse of the earth itself. This disconnection is not abstract—it settles deep into the tissues, into the levator scapulae muscles hugging the sides of your neck, tightening in silent protest, and into the diaphragm, which forgets to expand fully, leaving you with the shallowest of breaths that barely stir the ribs.

Maté speaks of trauma as the breach between your authentic self and the world you inhabit—a world often shaped by pressures to suppress your fury, your grief, your very aliveness. This suppression is not only an internal event; it is mirrored in the way society treats the land, normalizing destruction as progress, bulldozing forests, poisoning rivers, and severing the roots that bind you to the earth. When the soil is stripped bare, so too is your body stripped of the deep, embodied knowing that comes from living in reciprocal relationship with the world around you. The levator scapulae, the small but fierce muscles that hitch your shoulders to your neck, embody this disconnection. They seize up in defensive stiffness, a physical echo of the psychic and environmental rupture Maté describes. Your breath, starved of the diaphragm’s full engagement, becomes a whisper in the night instead of the full-bodied voice it was born to be.

This is not pathology, this is biology. The body knows what the mind might still be denying: that the “normal” you’ve been taught to accept is a collective holding pattern, a chronic tension that constricts both your flesh and your spirit. The levator scapulae don’t just tighten because you’re stressed; they tighten because your body remembers the ancestral ache of disconnection, the grief of land lost to greed, the betrayal of your own natural rhythms by cultural demands. The shallow breathing you carry is not a failing but a symptom—your diaphragm’s way of whispering that it cannot fully expand in a world where your own nature is constantly under siege.

Maté’s work unravels the myth that trauma is an isolated, personal tragedy and instead situates it within a larger, systemic wounding that includes the environment and the social fabric. Healing, then, is not only about soothing the individual but about reclaiming a relationship with the earth that nurtures you and the body that remembers. The tension in your levator scapulae and the breath that barely stirs your ribs are signposts pointing toward this deeper recovery—a reclamation of yourself from the false normal that has never served you. This is why fierce herbs, deep somatic work, and the slow, unyielding return to your own body’s wisdom are acts of radical resistance against a world that would have you believe your fury, your pain, your very breath are ugly or wrong. They are not. They are the first steps back home.

## Herbal Allies for the Uprooted

When you carry the weight of ancestral rage—a fury passed down like a secret inheritance, lodged deep in the sinews and membranes—the body doesn’t just hold that fire in your mind. It locks it into the masseter muscles clenching through midnight’s quiet, the psoas knotting as you shift your weight from foot to foot. This is not pathology; this is biology speaking in the language of tension and release. To navigate this terrain, you need allies who speak that language fluently, who understand that nerves can fray and muscles can tighten when rage is misunderstood or denied.

Milky Oats (Avena sativa) is one such ally. It’s not a gentle lullaby herb but a firm hand on the shoulder, steady and unyielding in its support. When your trapezius and levator scapulae muscles are clenched from carrying burdens not yours alone, Milky Oats soothes the nervous system with a richness that seeps into your fascia, quieting the restless hum of the sympathetic nervous system. The body remembers the grind of stress at dawn when the air is still cool and the scent of damp earth rises. Milky Oats encourages the diaphragm to soften, the breath to deepen, and the heart to settle into a rhythm less governed by fight or flight. It’s the herb that says, “You are allowed to rest even when the world demands your vigilance.”

Hawthorn (Crataegus spp.) approaches this rage with a different medicine, one that honors the heart’s fierce capacity to hold pain and hope simultaneously. When your chest feels tight, the muscles around your ribcage taut as if bracing for impact, Hawthorn works to open that space. It strengthens the heart muscle itself, improving circulation and encouraging a gentle expansion of the thoracic cavity—where so much of rage can turn to grief or unspoken longing. On a rainy late afternoon, the smell of wet leaves pressing into the soil mirrors the slow unraveling of tension as Hawthorn’s bitters slide down the throat, grounding the body’s frantic energy into something more manageable. This is a herb that knows the heart remembers every betrayal and every triumph, and it holds those memories without judgment.

Neither Milky Oats nor Hawthorn promises to erase your fury. Instead, they offer a way through the body’s response to that fury—supporting the nervous system’s capacity to hold power without collapse, to hold rage without fracture. They remind you that the jaw remembers, the psoas remembers, the heart remembers—and in that remembering, healing begins.

If you’re ready to meet these allies, here are trusted preparations to consider: [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) and [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20). Use them with reverence; these are medicines, not magic. The body knows what to do with them when you listen closely.

## An Embodied Practice for Grounding Rage

You stand barefoot on the cold hardwood floor, the late afternoon sun filtering through the window, casting a narrow golden stripe across the arch of your right foot. Your feet root into this light and the solid surface beneath—the dense, fibrous fascia beneath your soles awakening under your weight, like the first whisper of a storm. Feel the small, intricate muscles of the plantar arch engage: the flexor digitorum brevis, the abductor hallucis, the subtle spread of toes pressing into the earth. This is your foundation, your connection to gravity’s steady insistence.

Now, bring your attention to the pelvic floor, that undercurrent of musculature that supports you like the cradle of your own belonging. You might notice a dull, heavy warmth collecting there, or a tension coiling in the levator ani, a tightening that’s been waiting for permission to soften. Allow the pelvic floor to settle, not by forcing but by inviting—imagine the muscles as a hammock that gently sways with your breath.

Take a long, deliberate inhalation, pulling air deep into the base of your lungs, the diaphragm descending like a parachute unfurling inside your ribcage. Feel the lower ribs expanding sideways, the belly pressing softly forward as the diaphragm presses down into the abdominal cavity. Your trapezius and levator scapulae release their habitual clench, the neck lengthens, and your shoulders drop away from your ears—not limp, but spacious, held by your own skeletal architecture.

Exhale slowly through an open mouth, allowing the jaw—especially the masseter muscle—to relax, unclench the remembered tightness of fury lodged there. This is not a breath of calmness forced but a breath that honors the body’s language of rage. As you breathe out, imagine the energy of fury flowing down through the legs, through the calves and shins, past the ankles, and finally dissolving into the ground beneath you. The body knows how to move this energy if you give it room.

Repeat this cycle of breath and grounding, your attention tracing the lineage of tension and release from the soles of your feet to the crown of your head. This is not a practice of erasure but of embodiment—where fury is held firmly, not shamed or shoved away, but settled into the bones and tissues, recognized as an essential, fierce part of your living edge.

## FAQ: The Biology of Land Grief

**Q: Why does anger over land erasure feel so physical, like it’s lodged deep in my body?**

A: That fire you feel isn’t just metaphorical—it’s your body’s intelligence speaking in muscle and fascia. The jaw tightens as the masseter clenches, your trapezius and levator scapulae coil up like trapped lightning in the back and neck. This is not some abstract emotional reaction; this is biology. The body remembers the violence done to the earth the same way it remembers threats to your safety. When you witness land erased—forests clear-cut, rivers poisoned, ancestral spaces paved over—your nervous system reacts as if your very survival is at stake. This is not pathology, this is biology wired for care and protection. The diaphragm might clamp down, shortening your breath, because the body is bracing. Your skin might even smell faintly of smoke or earth, a visceral echo of destruction. These sensations are your body's way of holding the grief and anger so they don’t dissipate into numbness or dissociation. They are an invitation to listen closely.

**Q: How can I process this land grief without bypassing or numbing it out?**

A: You start by grounding in the body, not the head. Feel the fullness of your ribs expanding and contracting with each breath, notice the slow unwinding of the psoas in your deep core as it loosens its grip. This isn’t about intellectualizing or distracting yourself with activism alone—it’s about allowing the body’s wisdom to move through you. Herbs like skullcap or chamomile can soften the edges of the rage, not to dull it, but to create space for it to shift rather than get stuck. Move your body—walk on the earth if you can, feeling the texture of soil beneath your feet, or stretch deliberately into the muscles that hold the tightness. Your jaw remembers every clenched moment. Massage the masseter gently, or hum to shift the tension in your throat and chest. This is somatic grieving, not avoidance. You’re not bypassing pain; you’re giving it a container where it can be acknowledged fully without overwhelming you.

**Q: Is it okay to feel anger and grief so intensely about land erasure when so many others might not see it the same way?**

A: Absolutely. Your body is your own truth-teller, and it carries the lineage of your relationship to place, history, and loss. The intensity you feel is not an overreaction or a sign of weakness—it’s an embodied knowledge that can’t be dismissed. The levator scapulae, those muscles running up the side of your neck, might stiffen in response to the weight of this grief, and that stiffness is a testament to your connection. Other people might intellectualize, minimize, or even deny the pain, but your body knows what the land has lost and what that loss means for you. This anger is a form of respect, a refusal to forget what was taken. Holding this fire without shame is an act of radical care. It’s the body’s way of remembering and honoring what words alone can’t convey. You are not alone in this, even when it feels like a solitary burden. Your biology holds the story, and you have the right to feel it fully.
